title: Quiz: Monitoring Quantum Applications
description: Test your understanding of monitoring and observability practices for quantum software

questions.question: What makes monitoring quantum applications different from traditional software monitoring?

options.text: Quantum applications require metrics for probabilistic outcomes, fidelity, and hardware-specific characteristics
Quantum applications only need error rate monitoring
Traditional monitoring tools cannot be used for quantum applications

questions.explanation: Quantum applications require additional metrics beyond traditional latency/throughput monitoring, including fidelity estimates, circuit depth, hardware calibration status, and optimization convergence - all capturing the probabilistic and hardware-dependent nature of quantum computation.

questions.question: Why is monitoring calibration age important for quantum hardware?

options.text: Older calibration data may not reflect current hardware performance, affecting result quality
Calibration age is only important for simulators

questions.explanation: Quantum hardware parameters (T1/T2 times, gate error rates) drift over time. Stale calibration data means the transpiler and error mitigation may be optimizing for outdated hardware characteristics, leading to worse results.

questions.question: What type of Prometheus metric is most appropriate for tracking circuit depth?

options.text: Gauge - because depth represents a current value
Histogram - to capture the distribution of depths across executions

questions.explanation: A Histogram is ideal for circuit depth because it captures the distribution of depths across many circuit executions, allowing you to see percentiles and understand the range of complexity in your workload.

questions.question: What alert condition would indicate a VQE optimization is stuck?

options.text: Cost function hasn't improved significantly over many iterations

questions.explanation: A stuck optimization is characterized by the cost function not improving despite continued iterations. This can indicate hitting a local minimum, barren plateau, or numerical issues.

questions.question: Why should high-cardinality labels (like unique experiment IDs) be used carefully in Prometheus?

options.text: They can cause memory issues in Prometheus due to creating many time series
They slow down quantum circuit execution

questions.explanation: Each unique combination of labels creates a separate time series in Prometheus. High-cardinality labels (thousands of unique experiment IDs) can exhaust memory and degrade performance. Consider using summaries or cleaning up old series.

questions.question: Which metrics should be tracked to monitor quantum hardware backend status? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Backend monitoring should include queue length (affects wait times), availability (operational status), and calibration data like gate error rates (affects result quality). User's internet speed is not a backend metric.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'for' clause in a Prometheus alerting rule?

options.text: To specify how long a condition must be true before firing the alert
To schedule when alerts are evaluated

questions.explanation: The 'for' clause specifies the duration a condition must persist before the alert fires. This prevents alerts from firing on brief spikes or transient conditions, reducing alert fatigue.

questions.question: What metric would help detect if quantum circuits are accumulating too many errors?
questions.explanation: Fidelity estimates (comparing actual vs expected results) directly indicate result quality. A fidelity drop signals that errors are accumulating, whether from deep circuits, poor calibration, or hardware drift.
